summary: Paul Graham argues that smart founders often pick bad startup ideas because they grab the first idea they think of, avoid markets that feel scary, and confuse “cool” work with work that pays. The fix is to keep plunging, but learn to step back, discard sunk costs, and deliberately choose problems where customers will pay.
key_points: The “still life effect” makes founders stick with the first idea they start, even when it is weak.
Cool-sounding ideas often mask poor markets; unpleasant problems are more likely to be lucrative.
Fear of competition leads founders to pick impoverished markets, even when the scary markets are actually reachable.
Founders can learn to choose problems customers want solved; it is a skill, not a personality type.
Startups win by putting very smart people on mundane, immediately valuable problems.
money_quotes: So the biggest cause of bad ideas is the still life effect: you come up with a random idea, plunge into it, and then at each point (a day, a week, a month) feel you've put so much time into it that this must be the idea.
Work people like doesn't pay well, for reasons of supply and demand.
The purpose of a company, and a startup especially, is to make money.
It's hard enough to make money that you can't do it by accident.
That's the essence of a startup: having brilliant people do work that's beneath them.
key_insight: Smart founders must deliberately choose problems customers will pay for, rather than clinging to the first “cool” idea or avoiding competitive markets.
